content:
# Genesis Chapter 40 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 23r–23v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_040_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_040_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 38–39 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0042 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0043

---

*The Two Servants Imprisoned — Joseph Assigned to Them (vv. 1–4)*

**40:1** And it came to pass after these things [וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] — the cupbearer of the king of Egypt [מַשְׁקֵה מֶלֶךְ-מִצְרַיִם] and the baker [וְהָאֹפֶה] offended [חָטְאוּ] their master [לַאדֹנֵיהֶם] the king of Egypt [לְמֶלֶךְ מִצְרָיִם].

**40:2** And Pharaoh was furious [וַיִּקְצֹף פַּרְעֹה] with his two court officials [עַל שְׁנֵי סָרִיסָיו] — upon the chief of the cupbearers [עַל שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים] and upon the chief of the bakers [וְעַל שַׂר הָאוֹפִים].

**40:3** And he put them [וַיִּתֵּן אֹתָם] into custody [בְּמִשְׁמַר] in the house of the chief of the guard [בֵּית שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים] — into the prison-house [אֶל-בֵּית הַסֹּהַר] — the place where Joseph was bound [הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יוֹסֵף אָסוּר שָׁם].

**40:4** And the chief of the guard assigned [וַיִּפְקֹד שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים] Joseph with them [אֶת-יוֹסֵף אִתָּם] — and he served them [וַיְשָׁרֶת אֹתָם] — and they were in custody [וַיִּהְיוּ יָמִים בְּמִשְׁמָר] for a period of days [יָמִים].

---

*The Dreams of a Single Night — Joseph's Theological Question (vv. 5–8)*

**40:5** And the two of them dreamed [וַיַּחַלְמוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם] a dream [חֲלוֹם] — each man his dream [אִישׁ חֲלֹמוֹ] — in one night [בְּלַיְלָה אֶחָד] — each man according to the interpretation of his dream [אִישׁ כְּפִתְרוֹן חֲלֹמוֹ] — the cupbearer and the baker [הַמַּשְׁקֶה וְהָאֹפֶה] who belonged to the king of Egypt [אֲשֶׁר לְמֶלֶךְ מִצְרָיִם] — who were bound [אֲשֶׁר אֲסוּרִים] in the prison-house [בֵּית הַסֹּהַר].

**40:6** And Joseph came to them [וַיָּבֹא אֲלֵיהֶם יוֹסֵף] in the morning [בַּבֹּקֶר] — and he saw them [וַיַּרְא אֹתָם] — and behold [וְהִנָּם] — they were troubled [זֹעֲפִים].

**40:7** And he asked [וַיִּשְׁאַל] the court officials of Pharaoh [אֶת-סְרִיסֵי פַרְעֹה] who were with him [אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ] in the custody [בְּמִשְׁמַר] of the house of his master [בֵּית אֲדֹנָיו] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Why are your faces so troubled [מַדּוּעַ פְּנֵיכֶם רָעִים] today [הַיּוֹם]?

**40:8** And they said to him [וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו]: A dream we have dreamed [חֲלוֹם חָלַמְנוּ] — and there is no one to interpret it [וּפֹתֵר אֵין אֹתוֹ] — and Joseph said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף]: Do not interpretations belong to God [הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים]? Tell them to me, please [סַפְּרוּ-נָא לִי].

---

*The Cupbearer's Dream — Joseph's Interpretation (vv. 9–13)*

**40:9** And the chief of the cupbearers recounted his dream [וַיְסַפֵּר שַׂר-הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת-חֲלֹמוֹ] to Joseph [לְיוֹסֵף] — and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ]: In my dream [בַּחֲלוֹמִי] — behold [וְהִנֵּה] a vine was before me [גֶּפֶן לְפָנָי].

**40:10** And on the vine [וּבַגֶּפֶן] three branches [שְׁלֹשָׁה שָׂרִיגִים] — and it was as though it was budding [וְהִיא כְפֹרַחַת] — its blossom went up [עָלְתָה נִצָּהּ] — its clusters [אַשְׁכְּלֹתֶיהָ] ripened into grapes [הִבְשִׁילוּ עֲנָבִים].

**40:11** And the cup of Pharaoh [וְכוֹס פַּרְעֹה] was in my hand [בְּיָדִי] — and I took the grapes [וָאֶקַּח אֶת-הָעֲנָבִים] and pressed them [וָאֶשְׂחַט אֹתָם] into the cup of Pharaoh [אֶל-כּוֹס פַּרְעֹה] — and I gave the cup [וָאֶתֵּן אֶת-הַכּוֹס] into the hand of Pharaoh [עַל-כַּף פַּרְעֹה].

**40:12** And Joseph said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ יוֹסֵף]: This is its interpretation [זֶה פִּתְרֹנוֹ] — the three branches [שְׁלֹשֶׁת הַשָּׂרִיגִים] — they are three days [שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים הֵם].

**40:13** In yet three days [בְּעוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים] — Pharaoh will lift up your head [יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ] — and he will restore you [וַהֲשִׁיבְךָ] to your position [עַל-כַּנֶּךָ] — and you will give the cup of Pharaoh [וְנָתַתָּ כוֹס-פַּרְעֹה] into his hand [בְּיָדוֹ] — according to the former custom [כַּמִּשְׁפָּט הָרִאשׁוֹן] when you were his cupbearer [אֲשֶׁר הָיִיתָ מַשְׁקֵהוּ].

---

*Joseph's Plea to the Cupbearer (vv. 14–15)*

**40:14** But remember me [כִּי אִם-זְכַרְתַּנִי] when it goes well with you [אִתְּךָ כַּאֲשֶׁר יִיטַב לָךְ] — and show me covenant-faithfulness [וְעָשִׂיתָ-נָּא עִמָּדִי חָסֶד] — and mention me [וְהִזְכַּרְתַּנִי] to Pharaoh [אֶל-פַּרְעֹה] — and you will bring me out [וְהוֹצֵאתַנִי] from this house [מִן-הַבַּיִת הַזֶּה].

**40:15** For I was indeed stolen [כִּי-גֻנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי] from the land of the Hebrews [מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים] — and here also [וְגַם-פֹּה] I have not done anything [לֹא-עָשִׂיתִי מְאוּמָה] that they should put me [כִּי-שָׂמוּ אֹתִי] in the pit [בַּבּוֹר].

---

*The Baker's Dream — Joseph's Interpretation (vv. 16–19)*

**40:16** And the chief of the bakers saw [וַיַּרְא שַׂר-הָאֹפִים] that he had interpreted well [כִּי טוֹב פָּתָר] — and he said to Joseph [וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל-יוֹסֵף]: I also [אַף-אֲנִי] was in my dream [בַּחֲלוֹמִי] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — three baskets of white bread [שְׁלֹשָׁה סַלֵּי חֹרִי] were upon my head [עַל-רֹאשִׁי].

**40:17** And in the uppermost basket [וּבַסַּל הָעֶלְיוֹן] — from every kind of food of Pharaoh [מִכֹּל מַאֲכַל פַּרְעֹה] — the work of the baker [מַעֲשֵׂה אֹפֶה] — and the birds [וְהָעוֹף] were eating them [אֹכֵל אֹתָם] from the basket [מִן-הַסַּל] from upon my head [מֵעַל רֹאשִׁי].

**40:18** And Joseph answered [וַיַּעַן יוֹסֵף] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: This is its interpretation [זֶה פִּתְרֹנוֹ] — the three baskets [שְׁלֹשֶׁת הַסַּלִּים] — they are three days [שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים הֵם].

**40:19** In yet three days [בְּעוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים] — Pharaoh will lift your head from upon you [יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ] — and he will hang you [וְתָלָה אוֹתְךָ] on a tree [עַל-עֵץ] — and the birds will eat [וְאָכַל הָעוֹף] your flesh [אֶת-בְּשָׂרְךָ] from upon you [מֵעָלֶיךָ].

---

*Pharaoh's Birthday — Both Interpretations Fulfilled (vv. 20–22)*

**40:20** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] on the third day [בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי] — the birthday of Pharaoh [יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת אֶת-פַּרְעֹה] — and he made a feast [וַיַּעַשׂ מִשְׁתֶּה] for all his servants [לְכָל-עֲבָדָיו] — and he lifted up [וַיִּשָּׂא] the head of the chief of the cupbearers [אֶת-רֹאשׁ שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים] and the head of the chief of the bakers [וְאֶת-רֹאשׁ שַׂר הָאֹפִים] among his servants [בְּתוֹךְ עֲבָדָיו].

**40:21** And he restored [וַיָּשֶׁב] the chief of the cupbearers [אֶת-שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים] to his cupbearing [עַל-מַשְׁקֵהוּ] — and he gave the cup [וַיִּתֵּן הַכּוֹס] upon the hand of Pharaoh [עַל-כַּף פַּרְעֹה].

**40:22** And the chief of the bakers [וְאֵת שַׂר הָאֹפִים] he hanged [תָּלָה] — as Joseph had interpreted to them [כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר לָהֶם יוֹסֵף].

---

*The Forgetting (v. 23)*

**40:23** And the chief of the cupbearers did not remember [וְלֹא-זָכַר שַׂר-הַמַּשְׁקִים] Joseph [אֶת-יוֹסֵף] — and he forgot him [וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ].

---

## Textual and Philological Notes — Genesis 40

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 23r–23v**

Genesis 40 begins partway down the left column of folio 23r (page 44), following immediately upon the end of Genesis 39. The chapter setumah (ס) marker is visible between 39:23 and 40:1 in the Masoretic text, signaling the division between the two chapters even though the narrative is continuous. The Hebrew text in this chapter is particularly well-preserved, with full Tiberian vocalization (Ben Asher pointing) throughout. The folio image shows the typical three-column layout of the Leningrad Codex with interlinear Masoretic annotations. The accents (te'amim) are clearly visible, providing syntactic and cantillation guidance on the more complex verses: notably the accent pattern on v. 15 (the gunnov gunnabti construction) and the repeated yissa et-roshekha formulas of vv. 13 and 19.

Genesis 40 continues from 23r into the beginning of folio 23v (page 45). The chapter concludes at approximately the start of folio 23v, with Genesis 41 beginning thereafter. The chapter is 23 verses and presents no lacunae. The consonantal text and Masoretic pointing agree throughout with BHS. No ketib/qere variants of significant theological weight appear in this chapter in the standard Leningrad tradition.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 38–39**

Page 38 (pa_0038) carries the end of Genesis 39 and the beginning of Genesis 40 in the second and third columns of the three-column uncial layout. The chapter marker visible on page 38 corresponds to the Vaticanus enumeration of the Genesis chapter division in this section. Page 39 (pa_0039) completes the Vaticanus text of Genesis 40 and continues into the opening of Genesis 41 in the right column, where the chapter marker "CAP. XLVI" (or similar — the Vaticanus chapter enumeration differs from the modern chapter count) is visible at the right margin. The LXX text in Vaticanus for this chapter is fully preserved with no lacunae. The characteristic 4th-century uncial hand is legible throughout. The right column of page 39 extends into Genesis 41 material, and the chapter marker in the right margin for what Vaticanus designates as the next chapter division is visible. The Vaticanus LXX text of Genesis 40 preserves several notable expansions and clarifications relative to the MT, documented in the variant table below.

---

### Verse-by-Verse Notes

**v. 1 — chateu (חָטְאוּ) — "offended/sinned" — the verbal echo of 39:9**

The verb chateu is the Qal perfect 3rd plural of the root chata' (חָטָא, "to miss the mark, to sin, to offend"). This is the same root Joseph himself used in 39:9 when he refused Potiphar's wife: "how shall I do this great evil and sin against God [וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים]?" The verbal repetition is deliberate and significant. In chapter 39, Joseph refused to sin (chata') against his master and against God. Now, at the opening of chapter 40, the narrator describes how Pharaoh's two court officials sinned/offended (chateu) against their master the king. The irony is structural: the man who refused to sin is imprisoned alongside men who did sin — all three bound in the same house. The innocent man shares space with the guilty; the one who maintained moral integrity is indistinguishable in his physical location from those who violated their lord.

The LXX renders: ἥμαρτον (hēmarton) — the aorist of hamartanō (ἁμαρτάνω), precisely mirroring the Hebrew root. The LXX uses the same verb root for Joseph's "sin against God" in 39:9 (ἁμαρτήσομαι), making the echo equally audible in Greek.

The Hebrew phrase: chateu la'adoneihem (חָטְאוּ לַאדֹנֵיהֶם) — "they offended against their master." The preposition le- (לְ) in the MT is directional-dative: "they sinned to/against their master." Compare Joseph's phrase: chatati l'Elohim (חָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים) — "I shall sin to/against God." The grammatical construction is identical: chata' + le- + the lord/master who is offended.

**v. 1 — mashkeh (מַשְׁקֵה) — "cupbearer"**

The noun mashkeh (מַשְׁקֵה) derives from the root shaqah (שָׁקָה, "to give drink"). The mashkeh is literally "the one who causes to drink" or "the drink-giver" — the official responsible for serving wine to the king and for the safety of the royal drink. In Egyptian administrative context the royal cupbearer was an exceptionally trusted position: the cupbearer had intimate access to the king's person and was responsible for ensuring the wine had not been poisoned. This figure appears in Egyptian administrative texts and in comparable Near Eastern royal court documents. The LXX renders: ἀρχιοινοχόος (archi-oinochoos — "chief wine-pourer") in some formulations, and ὁ ἀρχιοινοχόος τοῦ Φαραω in the fuller title. The title appears again prominently in 41:9 when the restored cupbearer finally remembers Joseph before Pharaoh.

**v. 1 — opher (אֹפֶה) — "baker"**

The noun opher (אֹפֶה) is the Qal active participle of the verb aphah (אָפָה, "to bake"), meaning literally "the one who bakes." The baker likewise had intimate access to the royal table — responsible for the food that the king ate. The pairing of cupbearer and baker suggests a complete range of royal food-service officials. The offense of both against Pharaoh is not specified in the text — the MT is deliberately silent about the nature of the transgression, leaving the reader uncertain whether it was genuine dereliction, an assassination plot, or a court intrigue. The LXX adds no clarification.

**v. 2 — vayiqtsoph Paroh (וַיִּקְצֹף פַּרְעֹה) — "and Pharaoh was furious"**

The verb qatsaph (קָצַף) denotes a sharp, explosive anger — more sudden and violent than charah (חָרָה, the "burning anger" of 39:19). The LXX renders: ὠργίσθη δὲ Φαραω (ōrgisthē — aorist passive of orgizō, "to be angry"). The word is used of divine anger in Numbers 31:14 and frequently in the prophets, giving it associations of absolute sovereign displeasure. Pharaoh's fury triggers the narrative mechanism: both servants imprisoned, both dream, both encounter Joseph.

**v. 2 — sarisav (סָרִיסָיו) — "his court officials"**

The plural serisim (סָרִיסִים) here refers to high court officials — the same term as in 39:1 (seris Paroh, applied to Potiphar). See the note at 39:1 for the range of the term. The context here — the chief of the cupbearers and the chief of the bakers having these titles — strongly supports the administrative "court official" reading over the physiological "eunuch" reading, since these are clearly named functional titles (chief cupbearer, chief baker) within the royal court hierarchy.

**v. 3 — mishmar (מִשְׁמַר) — "custody/watch"**

The noun mishmar (מִשְׁמַר) derives from the root shamar (שָׁמַר, "to keep, to watch, to guard"). It refers to a place or condition of being watched/guarded — a detention under guard. The phrase "in the house of the chief of the guard, in the prison-house [beit haSohar]" specifies that this mishmar is located in the same beit haSohar (בֵּית הַסֹּהַר) of Genesis 39:20-23 where Joseph is already imprisoned. The narrative quietly notes that the place "where Joseph was bound" — making explicit the identity of the location. The LXX renders mishmar as φυλακή (phylakē, "guard/prison"), which is the standard LXX term for imprisonment.

**v. 4 — "for a period of days" (יָמִים) — the indefinite duration**

The phrase vayyiheyu yamim b'mishmar (וַיִּהְיוּ יָמִים בְּמִשְׁמָר) — literally "and they were days in custody" — uses yamim (יָמִים) as an indefinite measure: "some days / a period of days." The Hebrew yamim in this construction functions as an indefinite duration marker, unlike the specific "three days" that will dominate the rest of the chapter. The ambiguity is intentional: the reader does not know how long the officials have been in custody before the dream night. The LXX renders: ἦσαν δὲ ἡμέρας ἐν τῇ φυλακῇ ("and they were days in the prison") — preserving the indefinite plural.

**v. 5 — pitron (פִּתְרוֹן) — "interpretation"**

The noun pitron (פִּתְרוֹן) and its related verb patar (פָּתַר, "to interpret a dream") are characteristic vocabulary of the Joseph narrative, appearing only in Genesis 40-41 in the entire Hebrew Bible. The root may be related to Egyptian or Akkadian words for interpretation, or it may be a technical term developed within the Joseph story itself. The plural pitronim (פִּתְרֹנִים) appears in v. 8 ("do not interpretations belong to God?") — the only occurrence of the plural in the chapter. Joseph's ability to interpret (patar) is presented as a God-given gift — not technical skill or divination, but divine revelation (v. 8: "Do not interpretations belong to God?"). The LXX renders patar / pitron consistently with syngkrisis / syngkrinō (σύγκρισις / συγκρίνω — "interpretation / to interpret"), a technical term in Greek for the interpretation of omens and dreams.

**v. 5 — ish kephitron chalomo (אִישׁ כְּפִתְרוֹן חֲלֹמוֹ) — "each man according to the interpretation of his dream"**

This clause is unusual: it states that each dreamed a dream "according to / corresponding to the interpretation of his dream." The Masoretes point the phrase to suggest that each dream was shaped (or was shapeable) according to its own interpretation — i.e., the dream and its meaning were in correspondence. The phrase anticipates the interpretations: the dreams are not random noise but purposive communications shaped to their meanings. The LXX renders: ἑκάτερος κατὰ τὴν ἑαυτοῦ ὅρασιν ("each according to his own vision") — using horasis (ὅρασις, "vision/sight") for dream-vision, which is a slight shift from the MT's emphasis on pitron.

**v. 6 — zo'aphim (זֹעֲפִים) — "troubled/downcast"**

The adjective zo'aphim (זֹעֲפִים) is from the root za'aph (זָעַף, "to be displeased, to be downcast, to be angry"). It describes a troubled, dark facial expression — the visible external sign of inner distress. The LXX renders: ἐταραγμένοι (etarachmenoi — "disturbed, troubled, agitated," from tarassō, "to agitate"), emphasizing the psychological state of perturbation. Joseph's observation of their troubled faces is the narrative trigger for his engagement: he sees, he asks, he offers. The verb ra'ah (רָאָה, "to see") that initiates Joseph's perception in this verse is the same verb used of God's seeing in the Joseph narrative — Joseph functions, in his attentiveness, as a kind of mediator of divine perception.

**v. 7 — madua peneikhom ra'im (מַדּוּעַ פְּנֵיכֶם רָעִים) — "Why are your faces so troubled?"**

The adjective ra'im (רָעִים, "evil, bad, troubled") applied to faces means "downcast, troubled, ill" in appearance — not morally evil but visually unwell. The collocation penim ra'im (troubled faces) contrasts with penim tovim (good/bright faces). This is the only occurrence of the question "why are your faces ra'im" in Genesis. Joseph asks with pastoral attentiveness; his question models genuine care for others even from within his own imprisonment. He does not merely notice their distress — he inquires and creates a space for them to speak. This attentiveness is what surfaces the dreams and opens the providential path toward Joseph's eventual release.

**v. 8 — halo l'Elohim pitronim (הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים) — "Do not interpretations belong to God?"**

This is the theological center of the chapter — a rhetorical question that is also a theological claim. The construction halo (הֲלוֹא — "is it not that / surely") introduces a question that expects the answer "yes." Joseph is saying: "Surely interpretations belong to God" — the rhetorical question form makes it a stronger assertion than a bare declarative. The subject is pitronim (פִּתְרֹנִים) — "interpretations" — and the predicate is l'Elohim (לֵאלֹהִים), the preposition le- indicating possession or agency: "belonging to God / from God / in God's domain."

The claim dismantles the professional dream-interpretation industry of Egypt, where trained diviners (harashim, chartumim) derived interpretations through technical means. Joseph counters: the source of true dream-interpretation is not human technique but divine disclosure. He can interpret only because God will interpret through him. This is also Joseph's explanation for why he can offer to interpret without professional credentials: "Tell them to me, please" — he invites the dreams because he trusts God to supply the meaning. The same theological logic underlies his interpretation before Pharaoh in 41:16: "It is not in me — God will give an answer of peace to Pharaoh."

The use of Elohim (אֱלֹהִים) rather than YHWH is standard for Joseph's speech — as noted in chapter 39, the characters consistently use Elohim (the generic divine name) while the narrator uses YHWH (the covenant name). In the prison context, before Egyptian officials, Elohim is the appropriate theologically universal term.

The LXX renders: οὐχὶ διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἡ διασάφησις αὐτῶν ἐστιν — "Is it not through God that their interpretation is?" The LXX uses diasaphēsis (διασάφησις — "explanation, elucidation, clarification"), a word that is more explanatory/revelatory in tone than syngkrisis. The LXX adds "through" (διά) rather than a simple possessive — "through God" the interpretation comes, which slightly shifts the sense from ownership to agency/instrumentality.

**v. 9 — gephen (גֶּפֶן) — "vine"**

The vine (gephen, גֶּפֶן) is one of the most theologically and culturally laden agricultural images in the Hebrew Bible. Vine and vineyard are symbols of Israel (Ps 80:8-16; Isa 5:1-7; Hos 10:1; Jer 2:21), of blessing and prosperity (Micah 4:4 — "each man under his vine and under his fig tree"), and of joy. In the context of the cupbearer's dream, the vine producing grapes for Pharaoh's cup frames the restoration as organic and natural — the vine does what vines do, the cupbearer does what cupbearers do. The LXX renders: ἄμπελος (ampelos — "grapevine"), the standard Greek term, with no significant expansion.

**v. 10 — sarigim (שָׂרִיגִים) — "branches/tendrils"**

The noun sarig (שָׂרִיג) is a hapax legomenon — it appears nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible. The word appears to denote the shoot or branch-tendril of the vine. Ancient versions (LXX, Vulgate, Peshitta) agree on "branches." The LXX renders: κλήματα (klēmata — "vine-branches, shoots"), the same word used in John 15:5 ("I am the vine, you are the branches"). The symbolic reading of "three branches = three days" is one of Joseph's interpretive moves that will become the template for all subsequent interpretations: the visual elements of the dream correspond numerically and functionally to the future event.

**v. 10 — kephoreachat (כְפֹרַחַת) — "as though budding/blossoming"**

The simile keph + poreachat (כְּ + פֹרַחַת — "as/like" + "blossoming") uses the Qal feminine participle of parach (פָּרַח, "to blossom, to bloom, to sprout"). The vision is of rapid, compressed growth: budding → blossoming → ripening, all in one visual snapshot. The accelerated growth sequence mirrors the accelerated temporal resolution: three branches = three days, and within those three days the complete restoration will occur. The LXX renders: ἀνθοῦσα (anthousa — "blossoming, flowering," from antheō), capturing the floral image precisely.

**v. 11 — sachot (וָאֶשְׂחַט) — "I pressed/squeezed"**

The verb sachat (שָׂחַט) here means "to press, to squeeze out." It is used specifically for pressing grapes into juice. The root shares consonants with shachat (שָׁחַט, "to slaughter") but the Masoretic vowels distinguish them: here the pointing indicates the squeezing/pressing sense, not slaughter. The LXX renders: ἐξέθλιψα αὐτάς (exethlipsa — "I pressed them out," from ekthliō/thlibō, "to press, to squeeze"), capturing the winemaking gesture precisely. The verb occurs in this specific sense only here in the Hebrew Bible, making it a hapax in this usage.

**v. 13 — yissa Paroh et-roshekha (יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ) — "Pharaoh will lift up your head"**

This is the first occurrence of the pivotal phrase that will recur with devastating irony in v. 19. The idiom "to lift the head" (nasa' rosh, נָשָׂא רֹאשׁ) is an idiomatic expression meaning "to pardon, to show favor, to restore to honor." The act of lifting the head — literally raising someone's lowered, downcast head — is a gesture of elevation, reinstatement, amnesty. In the ancient Near Eastern court context, "lifting the head" of a prisoner or official signified their release and restoration to royal favor.

In v. 13, the phrase is straightforwardly positive: Pharaoh will lift your head = restore you to your position. In v. 19, Joseph will use the same phrase — yissa Paroh et-roshekha me'alekha — but add the devastating modifier me'alekha ("from upon you") — lifting the head from upon the body = decapitation and hanging. The same four Hebrew words carry life (v. 13) and death (v. 19), distinguished only by the addition of me'alekha in v. 19. The reader perceives the ironic double meaning only in retrospect: "lift your head" is first a promise of restoration, then a sentence of execution.

The LXX renders v. 13: ἀναλήμψεταί σου τὴν κεφαλήν (analēmpsetai sou tēn kephalēn — "he will take up your head") — using analambano (ἀναλαμβάνω, "to take up, to lift"), which preserves the ambiguity reasonably well.

**v. 13 — al kannekha (עַל-כַּנֶּךָ) — "to your position"**

The noun ken (כֵּן) in this collocation means "base, stand, station, position." The phrase al kannekha means "upon your stand/position" = "to your former post." The LXX renders: ἐπὶ τὴν ἀρχήν σου ("upon your office/authority"), using archē (ἀρχή — "beginning, rule, authority, office"). The restoration is specifically to the original position — not a new role but the exact role that was taken away. This specificity will be confirmed in vv. 21: "he restored the chief of the cupbearers to his cupbearing [al mashqehu]."

**v. 14 — ki im-zakhartani (כִּי אִם-זְכַרְתַּנִי) — "But remember me"**

The particle ki im (כִּי אִם, "but rather / only / if only") introduces a conditional plea — not merely a request but an urgent appeal: "only / above all else — remember me." The verb zakhar (זָכַר, "to remember") here is not passive recollection but active advocacy — the action that the cupbearer will or will not perform. The tragic irony of v. 23 — velo zakhar (וְלֹא-זָכַר, "and he did not remember") — echoes and reverses this plea. Joseph says "remember me [zakhar]"; the narrator closes the chapter: "and he did not remember [lo zakhar]." The word zakhar becomes the chapter's dramatic hinge.

**v. 14 — ve'asita-na imadi chesed (וְעָשִׂיתָ-נָּא עִמָּדִי חָסֶד) — "and show me covenant-faithfulness"**

The phrase asah chesed im (עָשָׂה חֶסֶד עִם, "to do/show chesed with") is the standard biblical idiom for acting with covenant-faithfulness toward someone. Joseph asks the cupbearer to extend to him the same loyal-kindness that would characterize a covenant relationship. This is the second occurrence of chesed in the Joseph narrative (the first in 39:21, where YHWH "extended chesed" to Joseph in the prison). Joseph's use of chesed here is striking: he is asking a pagan Egyptian court official to act toward him with the kind of committed faithfulness normally associated with the covenant relationship between YHWH and Israel. He is, in effect, asking the cupbearer to be to him what YHWH is: a faithful rememberer. The fact that the cupbearer will fail to do so (v. 23: lo zakhar, "did not remember") intensifies the contrast with YHWH's chesed in 39:21.

The LXX renders: ποίησον μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἔλεος ("do mercy/pity with me") — using eleos (ἔλεος, "mercy, pity, compassion"), the standard LXX rendering of chesed. Eleos captures the relational and compassionate dimension but, like charis in 39:21, loses the covenantal weight of chesed.

**v. 15 — gunnov gunnabti (כִּי-גֻנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי) — "For I was indeed stolen"**

This is one of the most formally emphatic constructions in the Joseph narrative. The infinitive absolute gunnov (גֻּנֹּב — Qal inf. abs. of ganav, "to steal") precedes the Pual perfect gunnabti (גֻּנַּבְתִּי — "I was stolen" — Pual passive of ganav) to form the emphatic absolute construction: "stealing I was stolen / I was indeed stolen." The infinitive absolute before a finite verb of the same root intensifies the verbal action to its maximum: not merely "I was stolen" but "I was truly, completely, without doubt stolen." This is the same construction as the celebrated gannov gannav in 44:12 and the various infinitive-absolute emphatics throughout Genesis (cf. mot tamut, "you shall surely die," 2:17; hefshed taphsid, "you shall surely destroy," 18:23).

The verb ganav (גָּנַב, "to steal, to abduct") frames Joseph's own account of his history: he was not a runaway, not a criminal, not someone whose imprisonment was justified. He was stolen — violently taken from his rightful place. This is the first time in the narrative that Joseph himself articulates what happened to him in Genesis 37 (the verb ganav was used by Laban of Jacob stealing away in 31:20 and 31:27). Joseph's use of the passive Pual — not "they stole me" but "I was stolen" — places him entirely in the grammatical position of the object, the acted-upon victim, with no agency in the transgression.

The phrase me'erets ha'ivrim (מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים) — "from the land of the Hebrews" — is the only occurrence of the phrase "land of the Hebrews" in the Hebrew Bible. This is not "the land of Canaan" but specifically "the land of the Hebrews" — Joseph's self-identification is ethnic and geographical simultaneously. He names himself by his people group, not by his patriarchal lineage (he does not say "the land of my fathers" or "the land of Canaan"). The use of ha'ivrim (הָעִבְרִים — "the Hebrews") in Joseph's own speech is significant: the term 'ivri (עִבְרִי) has been used by others about him (39:14, 17 — "the Hebrew man/slave," by Potiphar's wife); here for the first time Joseph uses it to describe himself and his people.

The LXX renders: κλοπῇ ἐκλάπην (klopē eklaphēn — "by theft I was stolen") — using the dative of the noun klopē ("theft") to render the infinitive absolute, a Greek device for approximating the Hebrew emphasis.

**v. 15 — bor (בּוֹר) — "the pit / the dungeon"**

This is the crucial lexical echo that connects three major narrative moments. The word bor (בּוֹר) means "pit, cistern, dungeon." Its occurrences in Genesis:

- **37:20** — the brothers say: "Let us throw him into one of the pits [habborot]"
- **37:22** — Reuben says: "throw him into this pit [habor] in the wilderness"
- **37:24** — "and they threw him into the pit [habor] — and the pit [habor] was empty, no water in it"
- **37:28** — Joseph lifted up from the pit [habor]
- **40:15** — "that they should put me in the pit [babor]"
- **41:14** — Joseph brought out of the pit/dungeon [min-habor]

Joseph's phrase in v. 15 — "that they should put me in the bor" — reaches backward through the narrative to the original pit of Genesis 37. The prison-house (beit haSohar) in which Joseph now sits was linguistically identified as bor in 39:20 and is here named bor again. Joseph himself makes the connection: the bor he is currently in echoes the bor his brothers threw him into. The physical geography of his suffering is unified by this word: from the cistern pit in Dothan to the prison-dungeon in Egypt, Joseph inhabits a series of "pits" (boroth). His plea to the cupbearer is not merely "get me out of this prison" but "remember that I am in the pit" — with all the narrative weight that word carries.

The LXX renders bor with λάκκος (lakkos — "cistern, pit, dungeon"), the same term used in the LXX of 37:20-24, explicitly preserving the verbal echo.

**v. 16 — ki tov patar (כִּי טוֹב פָּתָר) — "that he had interpreted well"**

The baker's motivation is revealing: he speaks only after he sees "that it was good that he [Joseph] had interpreted." The word tov (טוֹב, "good") evaluates the interpretation of the cupbearer's dream — the baker is satisfied that the interpretation was favorable (tov = positive outcome) and therefore volunteers his own dream, hoping for a similarly positive reading. He is naively optimistic — the reader may sense the irony, since the narrative has already telegraphed through the contrast in the dreams (vine vs. baskets with birds eating) that the outcomes will differ. The baker is seeking a good omen from a situation that already contains a dark sign (birds eating from the uppermost basket).

**v. 16 — salei chori (סַלֵּי חֹרִי) — "baskets of white bread"**

The phrase salei chori presents a translation challenge. The noun sal (סַל) means "basket" — this is unambiguous. The adjective chori (חֹרִי, from chur, "white/fine linen" or possibly "white bread") is more difficult. Ancient versions disagree: the LXX renders three baskets of kephourī (an uncertain transcription possibly reflecting a term for white bread or fine bread) and also uses an alternate rendering suggesting "wickerwork baskets." The Vulgate reads tres calathlos farinarios ("three baskets of flour"). The Targum Onkelos reads "baskets of wickerwork." Modern interpreters divide between "baskets of white bread/fine flour" and "wicker baskets" (baskets of a particular weave). The context — the baker carrying bread-things on his head — suggests the content interpretation: "baskets of white/fine bread" or "baskets of bread from white flour." The MT's chori most plausibly relates to the color/quality of the bread contents rather than to the basket material.

**v. 17 — makkol ma'akhal Paroh (מִכֹּל מַאֲכַל פַּרְעֹה) — "from every kind of food of Pharaoh"**

The phrase emphasizes the upper basket contains choice food — the finest of royal provisions. This detail makes the birds' eating from it all the more ominous: the highest thing (top basket, best food, the most elevated position) is the one that is consumed by birds. In dream symbolism across the ancient Near East, birds consuming human food is an ill omen — it signifies that what belongs to the person will be taken away. The association of birds eating from corpses (cf. Deut 28:26; 1 Sam 17:44, 46; Ps 79:2) gives the dream's imagery its sinister valence, which Joseph reads correctly.

**v. 19 — yissa Paroh et-roshekha me'alekha (יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ) — "Pharaoh will lift your head from upon you"**

The addition of me'alekha (מֵעָלֶיךָ — "from upon you") to the phrase used in v. 13 transforms the idiom from restoration to execution. The same three Hebrew words — yissa / Paroh / et-roshekha — that meant "Pharaoh will honor you and restore you" now mean "Pharaoh will remove your head from your body." The modifier me'alekha (from-upon-you) makes explicit that the head will be physically separated from the body — decapitation followed by hanging. This is the most chilling moment in the chapter's literary architecture: the identical phrase, with one devastating addition, pivots from promise to death sentence.

In Hebrew legal and court language, talah (תָּלָה, "to hang") on a tree ('ets, עֵץ) refers to the public display of an executed criminal's body — either hanging before death (cf. Deuteronomy 21:22-23) or impaling/hanging the body after execution. The public display by birds eating the flesh from the hanging body (vv. 17, 19) forms a deliberate contrast with the cupbearer's restoration: one serves Pharaoh's cup, the other becomes food for birds.

The LXX renders: ἀρεῖ Φαραω τὴν κεφαλήν σου ἀπὸ σοῦ (arei Pharaō tēn kephalēn sou apo sou — "Pharaoh will take your head from you") — using airō (αἴρω, "to lift, to take up, to take away") with the preposition apo sou ("from you"), precisely preserving the Hebrew distinction between v. 13 (simple "lift the head") and v. 19 ("lift the head from upon you").

**v. 20 — yom hulledet et-Paroh (יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת אֶת-פַּרְעֹה) — "the birthday of Pharaoh"**

This is the only reference to a royal birthday celebration in the entire Hebrew Bible. The phrase yom hulledet (יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת — "the day of the being-born of") is a Pual infinitive construct of yalad (יָלַד, "to bear/beget"). The Pual passive gives: "the day of [Pharaoh's] being born" = birthday. Birthday celebrations for Egyptian pharaohs are attested in Egyptian sources — they were occasions for royal beneficence, including the release of prisoners and the restoration of disgraced officials. This makes Joseph's anticipation of a reversal on Pharaoh's birthday contextually plausible: such celebrations were known occasions for royal amnesty and reinstatement.

The phrase also connects to the recurring verb yalad throughout Genesis — the entire book is structured around toledot (generations / those begotten). Here the pharaoh's birthday (being-born day) becomes the instrument of both death and restoration — the day of someone's birth becomes the day of the baker's death and the cupbearer's rebirth into his office.

The LXX renders: ἡμέρα γενέσεως τοῦ Φαραω (hēmera geneseōs tou Pharaō — "the day of the birth/origin of Pharaoh") — using genesis (γένεσις), the same word that gives the book its Greek title and appears in the LXX rendering of toledot throughout Genesis.

**v. 20 — vayissa et-rosh (וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת-רֹאשׁ) — "and he lifted up the head"**

The narrator now uses the same phrase — yissa et-rosh — in its narrative fulfillment of both interpretations simultaneously, without differentiation until v. 21-22. "He lifted up the head of the chief of the cupbearers and the head of the chief of the bakers among his servants" — both heads lifted on the same occasion, with the resolution delivered separately: restoration for the cupbearer (v. 21), hanging for the baker (v. 22). The ambiguity is maximized in v. 20 and resolved only in the following verses, giving the phrase its full ironic force in retrospect: "lifting the head" encompasses both the lift of honor and the lift of decapitation.

**v. 21 — vayashev (וַיָּשֶׁב) — "and he restored"**

The verb shiv (Hiphil of shuv, שׁוּב — "to return, to restore") is the same root Joseph used in v. 13: "he will restore you [vahashivekha] to your position." The narrator's use of vayashev in v. 21 fulfills Joseph's interpretation verbatim — the prediction (v. 13) and the fulfillment (v. 21) share the same root. The narrative precision underscores that Joseph's interpretations were not guesses but divinely given correspondence to future reality.

**v. 23 — velo-zakhar (וְלֹא-זָכַר) — "and he did not remember"**

The chapter's closing phrase — "and the chief of the cupbearers did not remember Joseph, and he forgot him" — deploys the two verbs of memory and its absence: zakhar (זָכַר, "to remember") and shakhach (שָׁכַח, "to forget"). The pair form an emphatic doublet of non-remembrance: not only did he not remember (lo zakhar) — he forgot (vayyishkachehu). The two verbs are not quite synonymous: zakhar is active memory that leads to advocacy; shakhach is the passive disappearance of someone from consciousness. The cupbearer failed at the first (did not actively remember = did not advocate) and was guilty of the second (let Joseph slip from his mind entirely).

This verse extends Joseph's suffering by two full years (41:1: "and it came to pass at the end of two years"). The period of forgotten waiting becomes the deepest point of Joseph's descent: innocent, enslaved, imprisoned, he now becomes forgotten. The man who interpreted the dreams of others has no one to interpret his circumstances; the man who asked to be remembered is unmemorialized.

The verb shakhach (שָׁכַח, "to forget") with the pronominal suffix -ehu ("him") — vayyishkachehu — creates a formal antithesis to the plea of v. 14: ki im-zakhartani ("but if only you would remember me"). The plea was for remembrance (zakhartani); the outcome is forgetting (vayyishkachehu). The gap between the plea and the outcome is the chapter's final ache.

The LXX renders: οὐκ ἐμνήσθη δὲ ὁ ἀρχιοινοχόος τοῦ Ιωσηφ ἀλλ᾽ ἐπελάθετο αὐτοῦ ("but the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, but forgot him") — using mimnēskomai (μιμνήσκομαι, "to remember") and epilanthanō (ἐπιλανθάνω, "to forget completely") — the Greek pair exactly mirroring the Hebrew zakhar / shakhach doublet.

---

### Structural Notes: The Double-Dream Architecture and the "Bor" Thread

**The Three-Day Pattern as Structural Spine**

Genesis 40 is organized around the number three and the phrase "three days":

| Element | Cupbearer (restoration) | Baker (death) |
|---------|------------------------|---------------|
| Dream symbol | Three branches | Three baskets |
| Interpretation | Three days → restoration | Three days → execution |
| Fulfillment (v. 20-22) | Restored on day three | Hanged on day three |

The parallelism is precise and deliberate. Both dreams share the same structure: visual symbol (three branches / three baskets) → temporal index (three days) → outcome (restoration / death). Joseph's interpretive method is identical in both cases: "This is its interpretation. The three ___ — they are three days. In yet three days Pharaoh will lift your head..." The shared formula highlights the divergent endings.

The symmetry extends to the narrative architecture: the cupbearer's dream is told first (vv. 9-13), the baker's second (vv. 16-19), and the fulfillment mirrors the order (vv. 21-22: cupbearer restored, then baker hanged). The chapter's symmetrical structure frames Joseph as the interpretive center: he is the hinge between dream and reality, between symbol and event.

**"Lifting the Head" as Double-Edged Idiom**

| Verse | Hebrew Phrase | Context | Meaning |
|-------|--------------|---------|---------|
| v. 13 | יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ | Cupbearer's interpretation | Restoration to honor |
| v. 19 | יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ | Baker's interpretation | Decapitation / hanging |
| v. 20 | וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת-רֹאשׁ | Pharaoh's birthday act | Both simultaneously (ambiguous) |

The three uses of the yissa-rosh idiom in vv. 13, 19, and 20 form one of the most controlled ironic deployments in Genesis narrative. The reader who reads v. 20 knowing both interpretations experiences a moment of suspended resolution: the same act performs opposite outcomes.

**The "Bor" Thread Across the Joseph Narrative**

The word bor (בּוֹר — pit/cistern/dungeon) traces the spatial geography of Joseph's descent and anticipated ascent:

| Reference | Context | Bor as |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| Gen 37:20 | Brothers plan to throw Joseph in | Empty cistern |
| Gen 37:22, 24 | Joseph thrown in, empty bor | The lowest point in Canaan |
| Gen 37:28 | Joseph lifted from bor | First ascent (to slavery) |
| Gen 40:15 | Joseph: "they put me in the bor" | Prison = the pit revisited |
| Gen 41:14 | Joseph brought out of the bor | Final ascent (to Pharaoh's court) |

Joseph's self-description in 40:15 — "that they should put me in the bor" — collapses the spatial history: the prison-dungeon of Egypt and the pit at Dothan are the same bor in his language. His suffering is one continuous descent into successive boroth. The vertical axis of the narrative (descent → pit → ascent) will be completed only in 41:14 when the verb alah (עָלָה, "to go up") reverses the series of yarad (descend) verbs begun in 37:25.

**The Memory-Forgetting Axis**

Chapter 40 establishes the memory-forgetting axis that will structure the two-year interval before Joseph's release:

| v. 14 | Joseph's plea: "remember me [zakhartani]" |
| v. 23a | Outcome: "he did not remember [lo zakhar] Joseph" |
| v. 23b | Outcome: "and he forgot him [vayyishkachehu]" |
| 41:1 | "And it came to pass at the end of two years" |
| 41:9 | The cupbearer: "I remember my sin today" — zakhar |

The word zakhar (זָכַר) moves from Joseph's plea (40:14) through the cupbearer's failure (40:23) to the cupbearer's eventual recovery of memory in 41:9. The two-year silence between 40:23 and 41:9 is the narrative space of forgetting. When zakhar finally returns in 41:9, it is the pivot that brings Joseph to Pharaoh.

---

### LXX Variant Table — Genesis 40 (MT vs. Vaticanus)

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|------------|-----------------|-------|
| 40:1 | חָטְאוּ — "offended/sinned" | ἥμαρτον — "sinned" | LXX uses the same root as in 39:9 (ἁμαρτήσομαι), preserving the verbal echo with Joseph's refusal to sin. |
| 40:1 | מַשְׁקֵה מֶלֶךְ-מִצְרַיִם — "cupbearer of the king of Egypt" | ὁ ἀρχιοινοχόος τοῦ βασιλέως Αἰγύπτου — "chief wine-pourer of the king of Egypt" | LXX adds "chief" (ἀρχι-) to the cupbearer's title, slightly expanding MT. |
| 40:3 | בֵּית הַסֹּהַר — "the prison-house" | εἰς τὸ δεσμωτήριον — "into the dungeon/prison" | Vaticanus renders beit haSohar as δεσμωτήριον (desmōtērion — "place of bonds/imprisonment"), differing from 39:20's ὀχύρωμα. The variation within Vaticanus reflects uncertain lexical tradition for the hapax sohar. |
| 40:5 | אִישׁ כְּפִתְרוֹן חֲלֹמוֹ — "each man according to the interpretation of his dream" | ἑκάτερος κατὰ τὴν ἑαυτοῦ ὅρασιν — "each according to his own vision" | LXX uses horasis (ὅρασις — "vision") rather than rendering pitron ("interpretation"), slightly flattening the MT's emphasis that the dreams were pre-shaped to their interpretations. |
| 40:8 | הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים — "Do not interpretations belong to God?" | οὐχὶ διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἡ διασάφησις αὐτῶν ἐστιν — "Is it not through God that their interpretation is?" | LXX uses διά ("through") rather than the possessive le- ("belonging to"), shifting from ownership to agency. Also uses diasaphēsis (ἡ διασάφησις — "clarification, elucidation") rather than syngkrisis. |
| 40:10 | שְׁלֹשָׁה שָׂרִיגִים — "three branches/tendrils" | τρία κλήματα — "three vine-branches" | LXX uses klēmata (κλήματα), the standard term for vine-branches (cf. John 15:5). MT sarigim is a hapax. |
| 40:11 | וָאֶשְׂחַט אֹתָם — "and I pressed them" | καὶ ἐξέθλιψα αὐτάς — "and I pressed them out" | Both render the grape-pressing action. LXX uses ekthlibō (ἐκθλίβω — "to press out"), which is accurate. |
| 40:13 | יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ — "Pharaoh will lift your head" | ἀναλήμψεταί σου τὴν κεφαλήν — "he will take up your head" | LXX uses analambano (ἀναλαμβάνω), preserving the "taking up" idiom. |
| 40:13 | עַל-כַּנֶּךָ — "to your position" | ἐπὶ τὴν ἀρχήν σου — "upon your office" | LXX uses archē (ἀρχή — "office, authority"), which is an interpretive clarification of the ambiguous ken. |
| 40:14 | וְעָשִׂיתָ-נָּא עִמָּדִי חָסֶד — "show me covenant-faithfulness" | ποίησον μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἔλεος — "do mercy with me" | LXX renders chesed as eleos (ἔλεος — "mercy, pity"), the standard LXX equivalent, losing the covenantal dimension. |
| 40:15 | כִּי-גֻנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי — "For I was indeed stolen" | κλοπῇ ἐκλάπην — "by theft I was stolen" | LXX renders the infinitive absolute emphatic construction with the instrumental dative klopē ("by theft"), an effective Greek approximation of the Hebrew idiom. |
| 40:15 | מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים — "from the land of the Hebrews" | ἐκ γῆς Εβραίων — "from the land of Hebrews" | LXX closely follows MT; the unique phrase "land of the Hebrews" is preserved. |
| 40:15 | בַּבּוֹר — "in the pit/dungeon" | εἰς τὸν λάκκον τοῦτον — "into this pit" | LXX adds "this" (τοῦτον — "this"), making the reference more deictic/immediate. MT does not have a demonstrative. |
| 40:16 | סַלֵּי חֹרִי — "baskets of white bread" | τρεῖς κανοῖ χονδριτῶν — "three baskets of coarse/fine bread" | LXX uses chondritos (χονδρίτης — "bread made of coarse/groats meal"), an interpretive attempt at the uncertain chori. Ancient versions diverge on the term. |
| 40:19 | יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ — "Pharaoh will lift your head from upon you" | ἀρεῖ Φαραω τὴν κεφαλήν σου ἀπὸ σοῦ — "Pharaoh will take your head from you" | LXX renders the devastating me'alekha addition with apo sou ("from you"), preserving the ironic contrast with v. 13. |
| 40:20 | יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת אֶת-פַּרְעֹה — "the birthday of Pharaoh" | ἡμέρα γενέσεως τοῦ Φαραω — "the day of the birth/genesis of Pharaoh" | LXX uses genesis (γένεσις) — the very word of the book's title — for birthday, a resonant choice given the Pual infinitive construct in MT. |
| 40:23 | וְלֹא-זָכַר שַׂר-הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת-יוֹסֵף וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ — "and the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, and he forgot him" | οὐκ ἐμνήσθη δὲ ὁ ἀρχιοινοχόος τοῦ Ιωσηφ ἀλλ᾽ ἐπελάθετο αὐτοῦ — "but the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, but forgot him" | LXX uses the adversative ἀλλά ("but") before the second verb, slightly intensifying the contrast. MT's waw-consecutive vayyishkachehu is more neutral. Both preserve the zakhar / shakhach doublet. |

---

### Key Term Lexicon for Genesis 40

| Hebrew | Transliteration | Gloss | Notes |
|--------|----------------|-------|-------|
| חָטְאוּ | chateu | "offended/sinned" | Same root as Joseph's chatati (39:9); verbal echo connecting the innocent with the guilty |
| מַשְׁקֵה | mashkeh | "cupbearer" | From shaqah (שָׁקָה, "to give drink"); trusted royal official |
| פִּתְרֹנִים | pitronim | "interpretations" | Hapax plural; appears only in Genesis 40-41; possibly Egyptian loanword or Joseph-narrative technical term |
| זֹעֲפִים | zo'aphim | "troubled/downcast" | Visible external sign of inner perturbation; Joseph perceives distress and asks |
| לֵאלֹהִים | l'Elohim | "to/belonging to God" | The theological center: interpretations are divine disclosure, not human technique |
| שָׂרִיגִים | sarigim | "branches/tendrils" | Hapax legomenon; vine-tendrils in the cupbearer's dream |
| יִשָּׂא אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ | yissa et-roshekha | "will lift your head" | Double-edged idiom: restoration (v. 13) or decapitation (v. 19) depending on me'alekha |
| מֵעָלֶיךָ | me'alekha | "from upon you" | The devastating modifier that transforms restoration into execution |
| כִּי-גֻנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי | ki-gunnov gunnabti | "for I was indeed stolen" | Infinitive absolute emphasis: Pual passive; Joseph's own account of his kidnapping |
| מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים | me'erets ha'ivrim | "from the land of the Hebrews" | Only occurrence in the Hebrew Bible; Joseph's ethnic self-identification |
| בּוֹר | bor | "pit / dungeon" | Connects prison (40:15) to the original pit (37:20-24) and anticipates the final ascent (41:14) |
| כִּי אִם-זְכַרְתַּנִי | ki im-zakhartani | "if only you would remember me" | Joseph's plea; the word zakhar becomes the chapter's hinge — unfulfilled in v. 23, recovered in 41:9 |
| חָסֶד | chesed | "covenant-faithfulness" | Joseph asks the cupbearer to do what YHWH has done (39:21); the cupbearer fails |
| יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת | yom hulledet | "birthday" | Only royal birthday in the Hebrew Bible; Pual infinitive of yalad |
| וְלֹא-זָכַר | velo-zakhar | "and he did not remember" | Antithesis of v. 14's plea; zakhar / shakhach doublet closes the chapter in forgetting |
